outcomes.statement: A person can sign in through their company's SSO identity provider, stay signed in while using Hearth, and sign out.
An org owner or admin can invite a teammate by email, and that person can accept or decline the invite.
Owners and admins can do org- and people-management things that ordinary members cannot, and the product enforces that distinction wherever it applies.
A member can create a task in a project, assign it, edit it, and mark it complete.
A person can attach a file to a task and download it again later.
The org has an audit trail of who-did-what that the people running it can review for compliance.
The right people are notified, in-app and by email, when things they care about change, and can see a feed of recent activity.
An organization can subscribe and pay per seat, and how many people it can have follows from what it is paying for.

requirements.description: Per-organization SSO identity provider configuration (metadata, entity/client id, signing certificate or JWKS endpoint, allowed domains) so each company's IdP can be registered.
Endpoint/flow that initiates the SSO handshake (SAML AuthnRequest or OIDC authorization redirect) with a generated relay/state and nonce.
Callback endpoint that receives and validates the IdP assertion/token (signature, audience, issuer, expiry, nonce/state match), extracts the verified identity, and provisions or matches the user.
User/identity record keyed to the verified external IdP subject (no local password), with email and profile.
Establish an authenticated session after a successful assertion (issue session cookie/token, regenerate session id to prevent fixation).
Middleware that validates the session on every request and resolves the authenticated principal, so a signed-in person stays signed in across the product.
Sign-out that destroys the local session (and optionally triggers IdP single-logout) and clears the session cookie.
Sign-in / sign-out UI: a way to start SSO sign-in, landing handling after callback, and a sign-out control.
Organization record representing a buying company (the tenant root).
Team record nested under an organization.
Membership record linking a user to an organization (and team), carrying join state, so the roster reflects who has actually joined.
Create an organization and make the creating user its first owner.
Create/rename/delete teams within an org and place members on teams.
Invitation record (org, invited email, inviter, role, single-use token, expiry, status pending/accepted/declined/revoked).
Owner/admin creates an invitation for a teammate by email with a chosen role.
Send the invitation email containing the single-use accept link.
Invited person accepts: validates token, ties acceptance to a verified SSO identity for the invited email, and creates the membership.
Invited person declines: marks the invitation declined and creates no membership.
UI to send invites, view pending/declined invites, and the accept/decline landing page.
Read the org roster and team membership (who actually joined).
Owner/admin removes a member from the org (leaving), revoking their access.
Role definitions (owner, admin, member) attached to org membership.
Permission/capability mapping that says which roles may perform which actions on which resources.
Assign and change a member's role (owner/admin can promote/demote), with guardrails (e.g. cannot remove the last owner).
Server-side authorization enforcement that checks the principal's role/permission and org-scoping on every protected action, everywhere it applies.
UI that shows/hides org- and people-management affordances based on the viewer's role (defense-in-depth, not the enforcement point).
Org-scoping/tenant isolation applied to all data access so a principal only ever reaches their own organization's resources.
Project record nested under an organization (and/or team).
Create a project within the org.
Project membership/access defining which people work in a given project.
Task record belonging to a project, with assignee, status (open/complete), and editable fields.
A member creates a task inside a project.
Assign a task to a person (must be a member with project access).
Edit a task's fields.
Mark a task complete (open to done).
List/read projects and their tasks.
Comment on a task (the comment activity the plan's feed references).
UI for projects, task lists/boards, and task detail (create/assign/edit/complete/comment).
File/attachment record linking a stored object to a task (filename, size, content-type, storage key, scan status).
Object storage backend for file bytes (e.g. blob store) keyed per org/task.
Upload a file and attach it to a task (validate type/size, store bytes, create attachment record).
Antivirus/malware scan of uploaded files before they are made downloadable.
Download an attached file later, served only to authorized principals (authorization checked on every download, scoped to the org/task/project).
UI to attach files to a task and download them from the task detail.
Append-only audit record (actor, action, target, org, timestamp, metadata) durable enough for compliance.
Write audit entries for security/compliance-relevant actions across features (join/leave, role change, work changes, file access, billing events, sign-in).
Owner/admin reviewable audit log view with filtering, restricted to authorized roles.
UI for reviewing the audit trail for compliance.
Activity event record capturing things that happen in the workspace (task assigned, invite accepted, comment, project update).
Per-recipient notification record with channel (in-app/email), read state, and the originating event.
Logic that decides who cares about each event and produces the right recipient notifications.
Deliver and display in-app notifications to recipients.
Deliver notifications by email (templating + send via email provider).
A feed of recent activity that authorized people in the org/project can view.
UI for the activity feed, in-app notification center, and read/unread state.
Background worker/queue that processes events asynchronously into notification deliveries.
Plan/price catalog defining per-seat pricing tiers.
Subscription record per org (provider customer/subscription id, plan, quantity of seats, status).
Integration with the payment provider (customer creation, payment method, subscription lifecycle).
Owner subscribes / pays: choose a plan and seat quantity and complete payment via the provider.
Idempotent webhook handler reconciling provider events (payment succeeded/failed, subscription updated/canceled) into the subscription record.
Enforce that active members + outstanding invites do not exceed paid seats; gate inviting/accepting when seats are exhausted.
Reflect team growth/shrink in billed seat quantity (update provider subscription quantity as membership changes).
UI to choose a plan, manage seats/payment method, and view billing status.
Authenticated application shell/navigation that hosts the product surfaces and routes only authenticated principals.

concerns.reason: The plan states the company's SSO identity provider is the only way in and there is no separate Hearth password; local password auth is explicitly ruled out.
The plan states this is greenfield with no existing product, accounts, billing relationship, or data to migrate.
Access is gated on company SSO and invitations; there is no password-based self-service account creation outside an organization's IdP.

mustHaves.statement: The IdP assertion/token must be cryptographically validated (signature, issuer, audience, expiry, nonce/state) before any session is created.
Session identifiers must be regenerated on sign-in to prevent session fixation, and sessions must keep a person signed in across the product.
Every non-public request must resolve and verify the authenticated principal before reaching protected logic.
Role/permission checks must be enforced server-side on every protected action, not merely reflected in the UI.
Every resource access must be scoped to the principal's organization so cross-tenant access is impossible.
Acceptance must bind to a verified SSO identity matching the invited email and consume a single-use, expiring token.
Every file download must re-check that the requesting principal is authorized for that file's org/project/task; download URLs must not grant access without an authorization check.
Uploaded files must be scanned for malware and only made downloadable after passing.
All compliance-relevant actions (join/leave, role change, work changes, file access, billing events, sign-in) must produce durable audit entries.
The audit log must be append-only / tamper-evident so the record can be trusted by an auditor.
Audit log review must be restricted to authorized roles (owners/admins).
Billing webhook processing must verify the provider signature and be idempotent so retried/duplicate events do not double-apply.
The number of people an org can have must follow from paid seats: inviting/accepting must be blocked once active members plus outstanding invites would exceed paid seats.
Role changes must preserve invariants such as never removing the last owner.
Sign-out must fully terminate the local session so the principal is no longer authenticated.
A task may only be assigned to a person who is a member with access to that task's project.

requiredEdges.why: Initiating the handshake needs the org's registered IdP endpoints/credentials.
Validating the assertion needs the IdP's signing certificate/JWKS and audience config.
The callback validates the state/nonce produced when the handshake was initiated.
A validated assertion provisions or matches a user record.
A session is only established after the assertion is validated.
The session is bound to the resolved user identity.
Middleware validates the session token/cookie that establishment issues.
Sign-out destroys the session that was established.
The sign-in UI triggers the SSO initiation flow.
The UI exposes the sign-out control.
Teams are nested under an organization.
Membership links a user into an organization.
Creating an org writes the org record.
Team management operates on team records.
Placing members on teams operates on membership.
An invitation belongs to an organization.
Creating an invite writes an invitation record.
The email is sent for the invitation that was created.
The email carries the invitation token from the record.
Acceptance validates and consumes the invitation token.
Accepting creates the org membership.
Declining updates the invitation status.
The UI drives invite creation.
The accept landing page drives acceptance.
The landing page drives decline.
The roster reads membership records.
Removal deletes/deactivates a membership.
Creating an org makes the creator the first member/owner.
Roles attach to an org membership.
Permission mapping is defined over roles.
Assignment sets a membership's role.
Role assignment operates on a membership record.
Enforcement consults the role-to-permission mapping.
Enforcement reads the principal's role.
Authorization needs the authenticated principal resolved by session validation.
The UI gates affordances on the viewer's role.
Scoping resolves the principal's org from their membership.
Scoping needs the authenticated principal.
Only an authenticated principal can send an invite.
Only owners/admins may invite; the action is authorization-gated.
Acceptance must bind to a verified SSO identity for the invited email.
Acceptance ties the invite to the accepting user identity.
The membership created on accept is assigned the invited role.
Removing a member is an owner/admin-only people-management action.
Changing roles is an owner/admin-only action.
Managing teams is a privileged org-management action.
Roster reads must be scoped to the caller's org.
Reading the roster requires an authenticated principal.
The invite management UI lives inside the authenticated app shell.
The invite affordance is shown only to owners/admins.
Reading the roster is org/people-management gated server-side.
Projects are nested under an organization.
Creating a project writes the project record.
Project access is defined against a project.
Only org members can be on a project.
Tasks belong to a project.
Creating a task writes a task record.
A member creates a task only within a project they have access to.
Assignment sets the task's assignee.
A task may only be assigned to someone with project access.
Editing mutates task fields.
Completion flips the task status.
Reading lists task records.
Comments attach to a task.
The UI drives task creation.
The UI drives assignment.
The UI drives editing.
The UI drives completion.
The UI lists projects and tasks.
Creating a project requires an authenticated principal.
Projects are created within the caller's org only.
Creating a task requires an authenticated principal.
Task creation is permission-gated to members with project access.
Assignment is permission-checked server-side.
The assignee must be a member of the org.
Editing is permission-checked server-side.
Completion is permission-checked server-side.
Task reads are scoped to the caller's org/project access.
Project/task views live in the authenticated app shell.
Task affordances reflect the viewer's permissions.
An attachment links to a task.
Upload creates the attachment record.
Upload stores the file bytes in object storage.
Scanning runs against the uploaded bytes.
The scanner reads the stored object.
Download resolves the attachment metadata.
Download streams bytes from object storage.
Files are only downloadable after passing the AV scan.
The UI drives attaching files.
The UI drives downloading files.
Uploading requires an authenticated principal.
Attaching to a task requires permission on that task's project.
Only people with project access can attach files to its tasks.
Downloading requires an authenticated principal.
Every download re-checks authorization for the file's task/project.
Downloads are scoped so no cross-tenant file access is possible.
File attachments are surfaced within task detail.
Writing audit events appends to the audit log.
Review reads from the audit log.
The review UI calls the read surface.
Joining (accept) is a compliance-relevant event to audit.
Leaving/removal is a compliance-relevant event to audit.
Role changes are compliance-relevant events to audit.
Changes to work are compliance-relevant events to audit.
Completing work is a compliance-relevant change to audit.
Access to files is a compliance-relevant event to audit.
Adding files to work is a compliance-relevant event to audit.
Billing events are compliance-relevant and must be audited.
Sign-in is a security-relevant event to audit.
Audit review is restricted to owners/admins.
Audit review only shows the caller's own org.
The audit UI lives in the authenticated app shell.
Notifications derive from activity events.
Fanout consumes activity events.
Fanout produces per-recipient notification records.
The worker runs fanout asynchronously.
In-app delivery reads notification records.
Email delivery reads notification records.
The worker drives in-app delivery.
The worker drives email delivery.
The feed reads activity events.
The notification center renders in-app notifications.
The UI renders the activity feed.
Assigning a task is an activity event source.
A comment is an activity event source.
A project/task update is an activity event source.
An accepted invite is an activity event source.
Fanout decides recipients from org/team membership.
Fanout targets the people with access to the relevant project.
Email delivery needs the recipient's email address.
The feed is scoped to the caller's org/project access.
A person only sees feed items they are authorized to see.
In-app notifications are shown to the authenticated principal.
The notification center and feed live in the app shell.
A subscription belongs to an organization.
A subscription references a plan/price.
Checkout selects a plan and seat quantity.
Checkout completes payment via the provider.
Checkout creates/updates the org's subscription record.
The handler reconciles events from the provider.
Webhook reconciliation updates the subscription record.
Enforcement compares headcount against paid seat quantity.
Seat sync updates the subscription's seat quantity.
Seat sync pushes quantity changes to the provider.
The UI drives plan/seat selection and checkout.
Only owners may subscribe/pay; checkout is privilege-gated.
Checkout requires an authenticated principal.
Enforcement counts active members against paid seats.
Outstanding invites count toward consumed seats.
Inviting is blocked when paid seats are exhausted.
Accepting is blocked when it would exceed paid seats.
Membership growth/shrink drives billed seat quantity.
Adding to the team (invites) is reflected in billed seats.
Letting people go is reflected in billed seats.
Billing management lives in the authenticated app shell.
Billing affordances are shown only to owners.
The app shell only routes authenticated principals.
UI gating needs the resolved principal and their role.
Creating an org requires an authenticated principal.
Subscribing is scoped to the caller's organization.
Commenting requires project access.
Team management is scoped to the caller's org.

_quadrantNote: Each requiredEdge carries selfRevealing/recovery/quadrant/quadrantConf (rubric v1.0). quadrant: lethal=silent+expensive, loud-exp=selfRevealing+expensive, cheap=selfRevealing+cheap, silent-cheap=silent+cheap. Authored by tools/author-hearth-quadrants.mjs; sidecar in quadrant-tags.json.
